chain_of_thought: Stack shows crash in __memcpy called from NI_PublicKeyDecode, then JNI bridge Java_com_nshc_NSaferJNI_N_1PublicKeyExport, then fuzz_one_input. This strongly suggests a memcpy-based memory corruption in NI_PublicKeyDecode.

Tools are unavailable (404) so I cannot decompile, but per instructions I must still reason based on given stack and typical patterns. JNI method name and NI_PublicKeyDecode imply decoding a public key blob supplied from Java/fuzzer. memcpy is likely copying from attacker-controlled buffer into fixed-size native structure without proper bounds.

Because the fuzzer is the harness entry and crash occurs in memcpy (not in explicit abort), this looks like out-of-bounds write/read. Absence of allocator/sanitizer frames suggests a direct SIGSEGV or similar. Real app JNI would similarly accept public key data, plausibly from network, file, or user input, so this is realistic, not just harness-only NULL misuse.

Even though I cannot see the exact arguments to memcpy or validations, the function name NI_PublicKeyDecode and classic crypto decoding bugs (assuming DER/ASN.1 or proprietary format) make an unchecked length/offset very plausible. The fuzz harness probably feeds arbitrary-length input, revealing the bug. In normal app use, malformed or oversized keys from an untrusted peer could trigger the same path, causing a crash (DoS) and potentially heap/stack overflow.

Given missing code, I cannot be 0.9+ confident about exact root cause or whether it is write vs read. But memcpy at top of stack, in a decode routine with attacker-supplied length, usually indicates CWE-787/805 style memory safety bug. I'll classify as a likely vulnerability with medium-high confidence and high severity due to potential for memory corruption, while noting uncertainty about exploitability details.
reasons: Crash occurs in __memcpy within NI_PublicKeyDecode, a decode routine likely processing variable-length public key data
JNI bridge Java_com_nshc_NSaferJNI_N_1PublicKeyExport is directly above NI_PublicKeyDecode, indicating untrusted/fuzzed input is flowing into native code
Fuzzer harness entry suggests no additional validation and reveals an out-of-bounds memcpy, which would also be reachable via real malformed or oversized keys
No evidence that this is a deliberate abort or environment-only misuse; memcpy faults typically imply memory corruption
recommendations: Review and decompile NI_PublicKeyDecode to locate the failing memcpy and add strict bounds checks on all computed lengths and offsets
Validate key blob length and structure in Java and JNI before passing to NI_PublicKeyDecode, rejecting oversized or malformed inputs early
Enable ASan/Valgrind-style instrumentation on libNSaferJNI.so to precisely characterize the out-of-bounds access and confirm whether it is read or write
assumptions: Public key data length or content is influenced by attacker-controlled or otherwise untrusted input in real app flows
NI_PublicKeyDecode uses memcpy based on computed lengths without robust bounds checking
The same code paths are used in production builds, not only in a special fuzzing configuration
limitations: Could not decompile NI_PublicKeyDecode or the JNI bridge to see exact argument handling
No explicit evidence whether the memcpy overflow is on stack or heap, nor whether it is read vs write
Lack of full Java call graph prevents confirming concrete external entrypoints and permissions

exploit.trigger_method: Supplying a malformed or oversized public key blob to a Java API that calls NSaferJNI.N_PublicKeyExport (or equivalent)
prerequisites: Attacker can influence or provide public key material processed by the app (e.g., certificate, key file, protocol message)
The app uses NSaferJNI.N_PublicKeyExport or equivalent path on untrusted or semi-trusted key blobs
libNSaferJNI.so is the same or similar build as in the fuzzing environment
exploit_pipeline: Identify an in-app feature that imports, exports, or processes public keys via NSaferJNI.N_PublicKeyExport (or related JNI methods)
Craft an oversized or structurally malformed public key blob that causes NI_PublicKeyDecode to miscompute a length or offset
Deliver the crafted key blob to the app (e.g., via file import, network protocol, or IPC) so that the JNI bridge is invoked with the attacker-controlled data
Trigger the operation that leads to NI_PublicKeyDecode and the unsafe memcpy, causing memory corruption and at least a controlled crash (DoS)
Iteratively refine the key blob shape to explore potential control over overwritten data for more advanced exploitation, if feasible
exploit.notes: Activity name and exact Java entrypoint for N_PublicKeyExport are unknown; replace <package>/<activity> with the actual exported/import feature activity. The crafted key blob should be derived from the corpus used in fuzzing that triggered NI_PublicKeyDecode->__memcpy.
